students.oneLineNeed: Aged out of foster care, forging a welding career.
students.story:
Wilberson entered foster care at the age of 16 and aged out of the system at 18, stepping into adulthood without the consistent support that many young adults rely on. Despite the challenges he has faced, Wilberson has never lost his hope, determination, or heart for others.

Anyone who meets Wilberson is immediately drawn to his contagious smile and joyful personality. He is an active volunteer at his local church, always willing to serve others and lend a helping hand. He enjoys being in community, loves riding his bike and driving, and has worked hard to earn his driver's license and purchase his own vehicle. Most importantly, Wilberson is ambitious and committed to building a better future.
students.aspiration: Today, Wilberson works part-time while pursuing an opportunity that has the potential to change the trajectory of his life. He has been accepted into a seven-week welding certification program that will equip him with the skills and credentials needed to begin a stable, well-paying career. This certification will open doors to long-term employment, financial independence, and a future filled with opportunity.
students.impact:
While completing the program, Wilberson will have limited ability to work, making it difficult to cover his essential living expenses. Support for housing, transportation, groceries, and other basic needs will allow him to focus on earning his certification instead of worrying about how to make ends meet.

Investing in Wilberson is more than helping him complete a training program—it is investing in a young man who has already demonstrated resilience, compassion, and determination. With the support of a caring community, Wilberson will have the opportunity to transform this moment into a lifelong career and build the stable, independent future he has worked so hard to achieve.

As a neurodivergent person, I am very aware that few people get opportunities like this sponsorship. I am not afraid to be pushed out of my comfort zone. This opportunity would help my family because I would use my learned skills to support myself and they would know that I could provide for myself. Who knows, maybe I could weld for my grandfather since he can no longer weld for himself. That would make him so proud.
students.aspiration: My goal is to be independent, self-reliant and self-sustaining. I know I have potential, and I strive to reach that potential. I learn best in supportive environments where people are patient and give me time to learn. I like the idea of welding because it requires creativity and is interesting. My goal is also to make people smile and feel at ease.
students.story:
My name is Thomas Russell and I am thrilled to be considered for this sponsorship. I was named after my uncle Tom, who is funny, loving, creative and always makes people laugh. I have followed his path in many ways.

As a young child, I was diagnosed with pervasive developmental disorder and later determined to be on the autism spectrum. This has presented me with many challenges. Thankfully, I have had numerous supporters along the way.  Teachers, mentors, family and friends have encouraged me and coached me through hard times which helped me develop endurance.

One of my values is humility. I have learned to look inward, which has led me to be self-aware and insightful. For me, open-mindedness is the key to moving forward. I believe in taking accountability and learning from my mistakes.

I come from a long line of enthusiastic learners. In fact, my grandfather has a passion for welding and was recently devastated to learn that he could no longer weld after having a pacemaker inserted. As a student, I am grateful, eager to please, curious and engaged. Developing a rapport with my teachers is especially important.

After high school, my family and I realized that I did not have a community and would benefit from an immersive program in an environment where independence is the primary goal. So, I came to 29 acres transition academy. This took a significant financial contribution from my immediate and extended family. Coming of age became easier in this environment and I have formed many friendships and bonded with my coaches and teachers.
